critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks a thorough literature review and empirical comparison with existing prototypical networks for NLP, making it unclear if Proto-Trex is a novel architectural innovation or a straightforward adaptation.
critique_points.point_002: There is no ablation study or theoretical argument validating the proposed non-gradient interactive learning design over gradient-based methods in terms of convergence, user effort, or explanation quality.
critique_points.point_003: The evaluation omits critical baseline training details such as pretraining status, hyperparameters, and model configurations, undermining claims of comparable accuracy.
critique_points.point_004: Experiments are narrowly focused on binary sentiment classification on two English datasets, lacking broader NLP task evaluations to demonstrate generalizability.
critique_points.point_005: No quantitative metrics or formal user studies are provided to objectively assess explanation quality, faithfulness, coherence, or user trust.
critique_points.point_006: The paper does not analyze how prototype representativeness, redundancy, or diversity affect explanation quality or model performance, especially under label imbalance or noisy data.
critique_points.point_007: Key details of the prototype layer architecture and hyperparameters are insufficiently described or buried in supplementary materials, hindering reproducibility.
critique_points.point_008: The interactive revision mechanism lacks precise algorithmic description and user interface details, making it unclear how user feedback quantitatively influences prototype updates.

summary: This paper proposes Proto-Trex, a prototype-integrated transformer model designed to provide inherently interpretable, case-based explanations for NLP classification tasks, and extends it with iProto-Trex to allow interactive human revision of prototype explanations without relying on gradient-based updates. Evaluated primarily on two binary sentiment datasets, the approach achieves comparable accuracy to baseline transformers, while enabling users to iteratively manipulate prototype explanations to better align with human knowledge and preferences. Although the method promises enhanced transparency, human-AI communication, and ethical reliability, the paper lacks comprehensive comparisons with prior prototypical methods, detailed evaluation across diverse NLP tasks, quantitative explanation metrics, reproducibility details, and user studies to fully substantiate its contributions and applicability.
strengths: Integration of prototype layers within transformer architectures to enable transparent, case-based explanations inherently linked to predictions.

Introduction of an interactive prototype revision framework (iProto-Trex) that allows users to directly manipulate explanations without gradient-based training, supporting human-centered model steering.
weaknesses: Insufficient empirical validation and comparison with existing prototypical networks and post-hoc explanation methods limits claims of novelty and effectiveness.

Lack of detailed baseline training configurations, comprehensive evaluations beyond binary sentiment tasks, and quantitative or user-centered assessments weakens the rigor and generalizability of the results.
questions: Can the authors provide ablation studies isolating the impact of the prototype layer on classification performance and explanation quality compared to standard transformers?
How does the interactive revision mechanism quantitatively integrate user feedback into prototype updates, and can this process be formalized with algorithms or pseudocode for reproducibility?
title: Interactively Generating Explanations for Transformer Language Models
